message.content:
Adversarially verify this finding about the repo at /home/jleechan/projects/ez-gh-actions (public: github.com/jleechanorg/ez-gh-actions, evidence at /home/jleechan/projects/ez-gh-actions/evidence/e2e-20260703, run https://github.com/jleechanorg/ez-gh-actions/actions/runs/28685531107). Try to REFUTE it by reading the actual code/evidence and, where relevant, live gh queries. Finding from "security" (major): Runner image field allows leading-dash argument injection into `docker run`, defeating all hardening flags
In src/docker_backend.rs:87 the configured image is appended as a bare positional (`cmd.arg(&cfg.runner.image)`) after all hardening flags and before `./run.sh`. `docker run` parses flags up to the first non-flag positional (the IMAGE), so a value that begins with `-` is consumed as an option rather than an image name. Because each value is a single argv element, curated flags like `--privileged`, `--network=host`, `--user=0:0`, `--cap-add=ALL`, `-v=/:/host`, or `--entrypoint=/bin/sh` all parse successfully; `./run.sh` then becomes the IMAGE and `--jitconfig <jit>` the command. There is no validation on `cfg.runner.image` and no way to insert a `--` guard (docker run does not support `--` before IMAGE). Attack scenario: an org-distributed or lower-privilege-written config sets image="--privileged"; the next spawned runner runs privileged with host device access, silently nullifying no-new-privileges, cgroup limits, and container isolation — the tool's entire security premise. The same class applies weakly to github target (src/github.rs:10-15) but that is always prefixed with `repos/`/`orgs/` so it cannot become a leading-dash flag (at most path-segment manipulation).
Refute if the factual basis is wrong, the scenario cannot occur, or severity is inflated (severity inflation alone -> not refuted, but say so).

content.content: [REDACTED for publication: verbatim content from a private repository (worldarchitect.ai self-hosted runner infrastructure) was returned by this tool call and has been removed. The reviewer's analysis of it is preserved in subsequent assistant messages.]

content.text: The finding hinges on `docker run` flag parsing and lack of validation. Let me check the config definition and how image is populated.
Both factual anchors confirmed. Let me check whether config is ever sourced from an untrusted/remote place (threat model) and whether any validation exists elsewhere.

input.command: cd /home/jleechan/projects/ez-gh-actions && ls src/ && echo "---INIT/MAIN---" && grep -rn "image" src/ && echo "---remote/fetch/url config---" && grep -rni "reqwest\|http\|download\|fetch\|url\|remote" src/ | head -30

content.text: The factual claim (leading-dash image parsed as a flag) is plausible, but the claimed *impact* — "runner runs privileged" — depends on `./run.sh` becoming a valid IMAGE. Note line 88 always appends `./run.sh` as the next positional. Let me test docker's actual parsing behavior.

input.command: which docker && docker --version 2>&1 | head -1; echo "---"; echo "TEST 1: image=--privileged, ./run.sh shifts to IMAGE position"; docker run -d --rm --name ezgha-test-inject --security-opt no-new-privileges --privileged ./run.sh --jitconfig FAKEJIT 2>&1 | head -5; echo "exit=$?"

content.content:
/usr/bin/docker
Docker version 29.4.3, build 055a478
---
TEST 1: image=--privileged, ./run.sh shifts to IMAGE position
docker: invalid reference format

Run 'docker run --help' for more information
exit=0

content.text: Confirmed: docker rejects with "invalid reference format" — no container starts. Let me verify a couple more injection variants and that no container is left running.

input.command: echo "TEST 2: entrypoint variant"; docker run -d --rm --name ezgha-test2 --entrypoint=/bin/sh ./run.sh --jitconfig X 2>&1 | head -3; echo "==="; echo "TEST 3: --entrypoint consuming ./run.sh, --jitconfig as next token"; docker run -d --rm --name ezgha-test3 --entrypoint ./run.sh --jitconfig X 2>&1 | head -3; echo "==="; echo "Any leaked containers?"; docker ps -a --filter "name=ezgha-test" --format '{{.Names}} {{.Status}}'; echo "(end)"

===

unknown flag: --jitconfig

Usage:  docker run [OPTIONS] IMAGE [COMMAND] [ARG...]
===
Any leaked containers?
(end)

content.text:
All three injection variants fail closed. Confirmed behavior:

- **image=`--privileged`** (or `--entrypoint=/bin/sh`): the hardcoded `./run.sh` at line 88 shifts into the IMAGE position, and docker rejects it as `invalid reference format`. No container starts.
- **image=`--entrypoint`** (consuming `./run.sh` as its value): the next token `--jitconfig` becomes `unknown flag`. docker errors out. No container starts.
- No leaked containers in any case.

Now writing my verdict.

input.reasoning:
REFUTED — the described security impact cannot occur; docker fails closed.

What is TRUE in the finding (factual sub-mechanism):
- src/docker_backend.rs:87 does append `cmd.arg(&cfg.runner.image)` as a bare positional after all hardening flags, and there is zero validation on `cfg.runner.image` (config.rs RunnerConfig.image is a plain String; main.rs Config::load does no validation).
- Docker's CLI does parse options up to the first non-flag positional, so a value beginning with `-` is consumed as a flag rather than an image. I confirmed this on the live host (Docker 29.4.3).

Why the finding is nonetheless refuted — the claimed OUTCOME ("the next spawned runner runs privileged with host device access, silently nullifying no-new-privileges, cgroup limits, and container isolation") is factually impossible:
- Line 88 unconditionally appends `./run.sh` as the very next argument, immediately after the image. So whatever single-token flag the attacker injects via the image field, the token that lands in the IMAGE position is always the hardcoded `./run.sh` (or, for a value-taking flag that swallows `./run.sh`, the following `--jitconfig`).
- I tested all three realistic variants live:
  1. `docker run ... --privileged ./run.sh --jitconfig X` -> `docker: invalid reference format` (./run.sh is not a valid image ref). No container starts.
  2. `docker run ... --entrypoint=/bin/sh ./run.sh --jitconfig X` -> `invalid reference format`. No container starts.
  3. `docker run ... --entrypoint ./run.sh --jitconfig X` (flag swallows ./run.sh) -> `unknown flag: --jitconfig`. No container starts.
- `docker ps -a` showed zero leaked containers after all three. Because the injected flag can only ever be a single argv token (the image is one string) and the immediately-following positional is the fixed `./run.sh`, there is no single-token value that both (a) parses as a dangerous flag AND (b) leaves a VALID image reference behind. Docker therefore always errors, `start_one` sees a non-success exit, cleans up the JIT registration, and bails — no privileged (or any) container is ever created. The tool fails closed, the opposite of the finding's "silently nullifying isolation" claim.

Threat-model point: this is not merely severity inflation. There is no org-distributed/remote config mechanism in the codebase (grep for http/download/fetch/remote/url config found none). The config is a local TOML authored by the same user who runs the tool and who already controls the docker daemon; that user could run `docker run --privileged` directly. Even if they set a leading-dash image, the realistic worst case is a self-inflicted DoS (their own runners fail to launch), not a privilege-boundary crossing or container escape.

Bottom line: the injection mechanism (leading-dash -> flag) is real and validating the image field is a reasonable defense-in-depth nit, but the finding's headline claim — a major security hole that spawns a privileged, isolation-defeated runner — does not hold: the hardcoded `./run.sh` positional forces docker run to fail closed, so the described attack cannot produce a running privileged container.
